one_line_takeaway: A 2024 taxonomy-and-objectives survey that reframes generative-AI unlearning as forgetting specific OUTPUTS under three axes (Accuracy, Locality, Generalizability) and organizes ~60+ methods into Parameter-Optimization vs In-Context-Unlearning families across image, text, and multimodal models, with privacy/safety/copyright/bias/hallucination as the driving security concerns.
key_finding: The field's central tension is the accuracy-vs-locality trade-off. Gradient-ascent (reverse-loss) methods achieve strong forgetting (Accuracy, Generalizability) but risk 'catastrophic collapse' of general utility; NPO is highlighted as provably slowing that collapse exponentially versus pure GA. Gradient-descent (no reverse-loss) methods best preserve Locality but forget less aggressively. Task-vector and parameter-efficient module methods can score well on Accuracy/Generalizability but the survey flags (via SSU) their instability under sequential/multi-round unlearning, hurting Locality. In-context unlearning is cheap and black-box-friendly but only alters immediate outputs without erasing internal knowledge, so unlearning is incomplete and recoverable. The survey's framing claim is that no surveyed method dominates all three objectives simultaneously.
core_contribution: This is a SURVEY (not an empirical/systems paper) of machine unlearning (MU) for generative AI, by Liu, Dou, Tan, Tian, and Jiang (Notre Dame / UPenn; arXiv:2407.20516, Jul 2024). Its OWN contributions, distinct from the ~68 works it catalogs, are threefold. (1) A re-formulation of the MU problem for GenAI: whereas classical MU tries to make a model behave as if retrained on D\Df (forgetting specific input-output MAPPINGS), the survey argues GenAI MU must forget specific OUTPUTS y_f (leaked privacy, harmful/toxic content, copyrighted text, bias) regardless of the prompt x, and it defines the forget set over a target-seen set, a retain set, and an unseen forget set. (2) Three evaluation objectives the authors themselves formalize as the organizing axes of the field: Accuracy (unlearned model no longer emits seen forget outputs), Locality (performance preserved on the retain set / unrelated tasks), and Generalizability (forgetting extends to unseen but related inputs). (3) A two-branch taxonomy of all surveyed techniques: Parameter Optimization (gradient-based with reverse loss e.g. GA/LLMU/NPO/Eraser, gradient-based without reverse loss e.g. PCGU/Fisher/EraseDiff/ESD/SalUn/Selective-Amnesia, data sharding e.g. SISA/FairSISA, knowledge distillation e.g. KGA/DI/delta-learning, extra learnable layers e.g. EUL/Receler/SISA-FC/SISA-A, task vectors e.g. Ilharco/SKU/SSU/selective-pruning, and parameter-efficient module operation e.g. LoRA-negation/Ext-Sub/security-vectors) versus In-Context Unlearning (parameters frozen; ICUL prompt/label-flipping and Larimar external memory). It spans three modalities (generative image models, (L)LMs, and multimodal LLMs) and five applications (safety alignment, privacy compliance, copyright protection, hallucination reduction, bias alleviation). Table 1 positions it against six prior MU surveys, claiming to be the first to cover all three modalities plus a full objective/dataset/benchmark treatment.
threat_model: As a survey the paper does not defend a single system; it motivates MU against the landscape of risks that GenAI trained on web-crawled data creates. The concerns it names as drivers: (a) privacy — models memorize and regurgitate PII (e.g. a customer's income, a celebrity's address/phone), assessed via memorization level and membership-inference success rate, with regulatory right-to-be-forgotten pressure from GDPR, CCPA, and APPI; (b) safety — models emit harmful/toxic/illegal content (the paper's Fig. 1 shows an unaligned model explaining how to steal from a cash register), including under jailbreak-style prompts; (c) copyright — verbatim generation of protected works (Harry Potter); (d) bias/discrimination; (e) hallucination. The paper also explicitly notes MU itself as an ATTACK surface: an adversary can inject malicious samples and then issue removal requests to degrade a model's performance, fairness, or unlearning efficiency (backdoor-like). It stresses retraining-from-scratch to erase data is prohibitively expensive, positioning MU as the practical mitigation.
limitations: Survey-level limitations: it presents no new empirical benchmark or head-to-head reproduction, so its per-family capability claims (Table 2 check marks) are qualitative syntheses of heterogeneous papers rather than controlled measurements. Defining the retain set D_r for GenAI is acknowledged as ill-posed given pretraining-corpus scale and diversity. The taxonomy is a snapshot of a fast-moving 2024 literature. Field-wide limitations it surfaces: catastrophic utility collapse from GA, instability of task-vector/PEM methods under repeated unlearning, and the superficiality (non-erasure) of in-context methods.

datasets_used: PKU-SafeRLHF / BeaverTails (safety, 834,000 task instances in Table 4; text notes 30,000 expert comparison entries)
Harry Potter (copyright, 7 books; not publicly available)
The Pile (privacy, 825 GiB from 22 datasets; Training Data Extraction Challenge = 15,000 extractable samples)
CelebA-HQ (image gen, 30,000 in Table 4; text cites 200K celebrity images)
quantitative_results: Survey compiles (does not itself measure) dataset scales: LAION 5.85B image-text pairs; The Pile 825 GiB / 22 sources; Training Data Extraction Challenge 15,000 samples; ICUL evaluated on 25,000 points each from Yelp and Amazon Reviews
WMDP benchmark: 4,000+ multiple-choice questions proxying hazardous biological, cyber, and chemical weapons knowledge
TOFU benchmark: 200 synthetic author profiles for selective forgetting evaluation
SISA-A adapters add only ~1-5% memory footprint, giving a claimed 95-99% memory saving versus full retraining (from Kumar et al.)

Table 2 qualitative capability claims: gradient-based-with-reverse-loss excels at Accuracy+Generalizability; gradient-based-without-reverse-loss and data-sharding and knowledge-distillation excel at Locality; extra-learnable-layers claimed strong on all three (Accuracy+Locality+Generalizability); in-context unlearning strong on Accuracy+Locality
Fig. 2 (from cited works, on LLaMA2-7B): compares GA, GA+Mismatch, SKU, Task Vector on Accuracy, Generalizability, and Locality-via-MMLU (illustrative, not authors' own run)
baselines_compared: Not an experimental paper — no baselines run by the authors. Prior SURVEYS positioned against in Table 1: refs [10], [94], [103], [134], [146], [176].

Method FAMILIES qualitatively compared in Table 2: Gradient-based (with/without reverse loss), Data Sharding, Knowledge Distillation, Extra Learnable Layers, Task Vector, Parameter-Efficient Module Operation, In-Context Unlearning.

Representative methods catalogued (cited work, not contributions): GA, LLMU, NPO, Eraser, EFUF, PCGU, Fisher Removal/Forgetting, EraseDiff, ESD, SalUn, Selective Amnesia, Forget-Me-Not, KGA, DI, delta-learning, SISA/FairSISA/LOO, EUL, Receler, SISA-FC/SISA-A, Task Vector (Ilharco), SKU, SSU, selective pruning, LoRA-negation, Ext-Sub, security vectors, ICUL, Larimar.
